scenario: BPMN has multiple connector types: Sequence Flow, Message Flow, Data Association, Association. Team wonders the distinctions.
question: Connector types in BPMN?

options.text: All BPMN connectors are identical and interchangeable -- this does not accurately reflect the behavior. 0 defines four distinct connector types with different visual representations and execution semantics. Sequence Flow drives token-based control flow within a pool. Message Flow represents asynchronous communication between pools. Data Association links activities to data objects or stores. Association is a generic connector for annotations and groups. Using the wrong connector type changes the diagram semantics and confuses both human readers and process documentation tools.

Arrow style in BPMN is purely decorative and carries no semantic meaning -- this does not accurately reflect the behavior. Each BPMN connector type has a distinct visual encoding that directly communicates semantics. Solid lines with filled arrowheads indicate Sequence Flow and token propagation. Dashed lines with open arrowheads represent Message Flow between pools. Dotted lines indicate Data Associations or Associations for annotation linkage. 0 specification and enable process readers to immediately understand the type of connection and its runtime implications without reading labels.

0 defines four main connector types, each with distinct visual notation and execution semantics: Sequence Flow (solid line, filled arrowhead) drives token-based control flow within a pool; Message Flow (dashed line, open arrowhead) represents asynchronous message exchange between separate pools; Data Association (dotted line, open arrowhead) links activities to Data Objects or Data Stores; and Association (dotted line, optional arrow) connects annotations and groups to process elements without affecting execution. Using the correct connector type ensures both correct runtime behavior and accurate process documentation.

a.text: Incorrect. BPMN connector types: Sequence Flow (per Set 16 Q1): Visual: solid line, filled arrowhead. Purpose: control flow between activities/gateways/events within same pool. Engine impact: drives execution; tokens flow. Examples: ` [Task A] ──> [Task B] Message Flow: Visual: dashed line, open arrowhead. Purpose: message exchange between pools (participants). Engine impact: typically documentary; actual message via Message events. Examples: [Send Order] ─ ─ ─> [Receive Order] Customer Pool Bank Pool Data Association: Visual: dotted line, open arrowhead. Purpose: connect activities to Data Objects or Data Stores. Engine impact: documentary (Camunda 8 uses variables + Document Handling for actual data). Examples: [Task] ┄┄┄> [Data Object: Invoice] Activity Output association Association: Visual: dotted line, optional arrowhead. Purpose: generic; connects annotations, groups, text to elements. Engine impact: none; documentary. Examples: [Task] ┄┄┄ [Text Annotation: "Sends email"] ` Annotation attached. Visual hierarchy (more solid = stronger semantic): | Connector | Visual | Engine | Direction | |-----------|--------|--------|-----------| | Sequence Flow | Solid | Drives flow | Within pool | | Message Flow | Dashed | Documentary | Across pools | | Data Association | Dotted with arrow | Documentary | Activity ↔ Data | | Association | Dotted | Documentary | Free | For Camunda 8 execution: Sequence Flow: actually drives engine. Others: documentary; affect diagram only. For modelling: When to use Sequence Flow: Always within pool for control flow. When to use Message Flow: Cross-pool communication. Collaboration diagrams. When to use Data Association: Show data inputs / outputs of activities. Documentation. When to use Association: Attach annotations, notes. Connect Group elements (per Q6). Common mistakes: Mistake 1: Sequence Flow across pools: Invalid: must be Message Flow. Mistake 2: Message Flow within pool: Unusual: typically Sequence Flow. Some interpretations: invalid; redundant. Mistake 3: Solid line where dotted should be: Confuses readers; wrong semantic. For validation: BPMN validators: catch wrong connector types. bpmn-js + lint (per Set 14 Q59): enforce rules. For documentation purposes: All connector types: can appear in detailed diagrams. Execution-only diagrams: may omit data / association. For collaboration diagrams: Multiple pools: Message Flow shows interactions. Internal: Sequence Flow. For data flow diagrams: Data Associations: data movement. Activities consume / produce. For annotation richness: Group + Association: visual category. Text annotation + Association: notes. Combine for documentation. For modelers' editor: Web Modeler / Desktop Modeler: pick connector type. Validation: prevent wrong types. For BPMN linting tools: Rule: correct connector for context. Catch mistakes automatically. Anti-patterns: Wrong connector type for context: confuses semantics. Inconsistent: across diagrams. Best practices: Use correct connector type: per BPMN spec. Consistency: across project. Validate: with tools. For the scenario (multiple connector types): Each has distinct purpose. Use appropriately.

findings.message: option text contains its own verdict/rationale
